core_story.summary: To address the above problem, several works [6, 10, 18, 29, 37] have proposed to construct a 3D V AE with both spatial and temporal compression capabilities, which can be summarized in the following three points. Unfortunately, this method struggles to effectively eliminate temporal redundancy, limiting the LVDMs to per- ceive and generate long sequences of video content. Abstract Variational Autoencoder (VAE) aims to compress pixel data into low-dimensional latent space, playing an important role in OpenAI’s Sora and other latent video diffusion gen- eration models. To alleviate these problems, we propose a keyframe-based temporal compression (KTC) ar- chitecture and a group causal convolution (GCConv) mod- ule to further improve video VAE (IV-VAE). Extensive ex- periments on five benchmarks demonstrate the SOTA video reconstruction and generation abilities of our IV-VAE.

1. Introduction
In recent years, video generation has made significant
advances in both academia and industry, showcasing
cinematic-level visuals and the potential of a world simu-
lator (e.g., Sora [16]). In particular, diffusion optimization
methods on latent space have garnered widespread attention
and dominated the video generation field due to their high
efficiency and stability, resulting in a series of outstanding

dimensional pixel space and the low-dimensional latent
space, Variational Autoencoder (V AE) [13] is employed to
encode the original video continuously into the latent space,
along with spatial and even temporal compression. The
compression rate and reconstruction quality of V AE directly
determine the information effectiveness of the video corpus,
playing an important role in the exploration of LVDMs.

method_expression: To alleviate these problems, we propose a keyframe-based temporal compression (KTC) ar- chitecture and a group causal convolution (GCConv) mod- ule to further improve video VAE (IV-VAE).

To this end, we propose a dual-branch keyframe-based temporal compres- sion architecture, where a 2D branch inheriting the low- dimensional image V AE prior focuses on keyframe com- pression, and a 3D branch facilitates temporal compression.
Experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art video recon- struction at various resolutions and motion rates.

Abstract Variational Autoencoder (VAE) aims to compress pixel data into low-dimensional latent space, playing an important role in OpenAI’s Sora and other latent video diffusion gen- eration models.

Conclusion In this paper, we observe that regular initialization of utiliz- ing image V AE weights with the same latent channel num- ber is not an optimal scheme, and the use of causal convo- lution leads to performance imbalance between frames.

Due to the restriction of for- ward flow of causal convolution, the first frame in the frame group is unable to perceive the other frames until after time compression in the middle layer of the network.

Experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art video recon- struction at various resolutions and motion rates.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Variational Autoencoder (VAE) aims to compress pixel data
into low-dimensional latent space, playing an important
role in OpenAI’s Sora and other latent video diffusion gen-
eration models. While most existing video VAEs inflate
a pre-trained image VAE into the 3D causal structure for
temporal-spatial compression, this paper presents two as-
tonishing findings: (1) The initialization from a well-trained
image VAE with the same latent dimensions is not an opti-
mal scheme. (2) The adoption of causal reasoning leads
to unequal information interactions and unbalanced per-
formance between frames. To alleviate these problems, we
propose a keyframe-based temporal compression (KTC) ar-
chitecture and a group causal convolution (GCConv) mod-
ule to further improve video VAE (IV-VAE). Specifically, the
KTC architecture divides the latent space into two branches,
in which one half completely inherits the compression prior
of keyframes from a lower-dimension image VAE while the
other half involves temporal compression through 3D group
causal convolution, reducing temporal-spatial conflicts and
accelerating the convergence speed of video VAE. The GC-
Conv in the above 3D half uses standard convolution within
each frame group to ensure inter-frame equivalence, and
employs causal logical padding between groups to maintain
flexibility in processing variable frame video. Extensive ex-
periments on five benchmarks demonstrate the SOTA video
reconstruction and generation abilities of our IV-VAE.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
In recent years, video generation has made significant
advances in both academia and industry, showcasing
cinematic-level visuals and the potential of a world simu-
lator (e.g., Sora [16]). In particular, diffusion optimization
methods on latent space have garnered widespread attention
and dominated the video generation field due to their high
efficiency and stability, resulting in a series of outstanding
*Kai Zhu and Wei Zhai are the corresponding authors.
Latent Video Diffusion Models (LVDMs) [1, 10, 12, 32].
To achieve a bidirectional mapping between the high-
dimensional pixel space and the low-dimensional latent
space, Variational Autoencoder (V AE) [13] is employed to
encode the original video continuously into the latent space,
along with spatial and even temporal compression. The
compression rate and reconstruction quality of V AE directly
determine the information effectiveness of the video corpus,
playing an important role in the exploration of LVDMs.
As the most commonly used video V AE, SVD-V AE [1]
freezes the weights of the 2D encoder based on the image
V AE [19] and retrains a temporal decoder containing 3D
convolutional blocks, which enables the feature interaction
across different frames and significantly reduces flicker ar-
tifacts. Unfortunately, this method struggles to effectively
eliminate temporal redundancy, limiting the LVDMs to per-
ceive and generate long sequences of video content.
To address the above problem, several works [6, 10, 18,
29, 37] have proposed to construct a 3D V AE with both
spatial and temporal compression capabilities, which can
be summarized in the following three points. In terms of
model structure, the UNet network [20] with strong pixel-
perception capabilities is still adopted, consistent with the
classic image V AE series (i.e., Stable Diffusion V AE). For
the operator implementation, most of the methods [6, 10,
18, 29] adopt 3D causal convolution with only forward se-
quence correlation, which can encode the image informa-
tion independently [31] while ensuring the continuity of
the long sequence video encoding. During the optimiza-
tion process, a well-trained 2D image V AE, focusing only
on spatial compression, is used as an initialization for 3D
video V AEs with the same structure and equivalent latent
channel number [6, 10, 18, 37], which can achieve better
results compared to training a temporal-spatial compressed
video V AE from scratch [30]. Although existing methods
achieve video reconstruction under 4 × time compression,
we find that there remain deficiencies in the above three
key processes, limiting the improvement of the video qual-
ity and continuity in reconstruction results.
First, initialization from a well-trained image V AE with

Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.

Figure 1. (a) Information preservation degree for different V AEs on video data. CR: Compression Rate. (b) Performance of different
frames within a frame group on kinetics-600 dataset (OD-V AE [6], OS (OpenSora V1.2)-V AE [10], CogX (CogVideoX)-V AE [29]).
the same latent channel number cannot support further 4 ×
time compression, leading to a sharp decline in the initial
spatial compression performance and slow learning of sub-
sequent temporal compression. As shown in Fig. 1(a), we
calculate the reconstruction similarity of different V AE on
video data as the information preservation degree (See Sup-
plementary Material). We note that: (1) As the latent chan-
nel number Z increases, the spatial compression capacity
gain produced by the image V AE gradually diminishes (blue
dashed line) (2) The spatial compression capability pro-
vided by an image V AE with dimensionZ/2 is sufficient for
a temporal-spatial compressed video V AE with dimension
Z. Then the video V AE can use the remaining latent chan-
nels to store the spatial information of extra frames, i.e.,
acquiring the initial 2 × time compression capacity, which
(red curves) converges even better and faster than the regu-
lar initialization approach [6, 10, 18, 37] that inherits the im-
age V AE weights of the same dimensionZ (green curves).
This inspires us to start from an image prior with a lower
latent channel number and leave the remaining latent chan-
nels for temporal compression, promoting a more balanced
temporal-spatial compression initialization. To this end, we
propose a dual-branch keyframe-based temporal compres-
sion architecture, where a 2D branch inheriting the low-
dimensional image V AE prior focuses on keyframe com-
pression, and a 3D branch facilitates temporal compression.
Second, the unidirectional information flow of 3D causal
convolution prevents the interaction between video frames,
exacerbating inter-frame unbalanced performance under
time compression. In the forward process, M frames (a
frame group) are compressed to a whole (one frame) and
then restored to M frames. Due to the restriction of for-
ward flow of causal convolution, the first frame in the frame
group is unable to perceive the other frames until after time
compression in the middle layer of the network. Thus
the first frame has the poorest ability to interact with the
whole frame group, resulting in higher optimization diffi-
culty compared to other frames. As shown in Fig. 1(b),
the methods [6, 10, 29] based on causal convolution suffers
from poor performance of the first frame in the frame group
and large fluctuations in SSIM values between frames, lead-
ing to significant flickering in the reconstruction results.
This inspires us to propose group causal convolution, which
groups the input frames based on the temporal compression
rate. Causal logic padding operations are applied between
frame groups to ensure the continuity in processing variable
frame videos, and standard convolution operations are used
within each group of M frames to share equivalent infor-
mation interactions, achieving smoother reconstruction.
In addition, the local receptive field struggles to meet
the temporal compression demand at high resolutions. For
the same video, the higher the resolution, the more pixels
the same motion spans, making it more challenging for the
model to capture motion patterns. To this end, we introduce
atrous convolutions and add multiple attention modules to
expand the receptive field, thus improving the temporal mo-
tion perception of the model at high resolutions.
Finally, considering the increasing demand for content
diversity and high resolution in the video generation field,
there is a lack of a suitable open-source benchmark for com-
prehensive evaluation of video V AE reconstruction capa-
bilities. Most of the datasets ( e.g., Kinetics-600 [4] and
UCF-101 [21]) are 720P or lower, and cannot be used for
high resolution (e.g., 1080P) evaluations. Moreover, high-
resolution datasets (e.g., Panda-70M [8]) consist mainly of
slow-motion and fixed-shot videos, which hardly reflects
the overall performance of video V AEs under varying mo-
tion speeds. Therefore, we re-collect a subset of 2000
1080P videos from OpenVid-0.4M [15] dataset, uniformly
containing various motion speeds, named MotionHD, as a
supplement to the overall evaluation. Experimental results
show that our method achieves state-of-the-art video recon-
struction at various resolutions and motion rates.
In summary, the contributions of this paper include: (1)
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we observe that regular initialization of utiliz-
ing image V AE weights with the same latent channel num-
ber is not an optimal scheme, and the use of causal convo-
lution leads to performance imbalance between frames. To
address these problems, we propose a keyframe-based tem-
poral compression architecture to accelerate convergence
speed by providing more balanced and powerful temporal-
spatial compression initialization. In addition, group causal
convolution is introduced to achieve better and more bal-
anced performance between frames. Extensive experiments
show that IV-V AE achieves SOTA results in both video re-
construction and generation.
